cards.prompt: Define Artificial intelligence in AI and give its practical purpose.
cards.answer: AI is software or machines performing tasks that normally require human intelligence, such as understanding language, recognizing images, planning, reasoning, or generating content.

cards.prompt: Define Machine learning in AI and give its practical purpose.
cards.answer: Machine learning is a subfield of AI where systems learn patterns from data instead of being explicitly programmed for every rule.

cards.prompt: Define Deep learning in AI and give its practical purpose.
cards.answer: Deep learning uses multi-layer neural networks to learn representations from large amounts of data, often powering vision, speech, and modern language models.

cards.prompt: Define Generative AI in AI and give its practical purpose.
cards.answer: Generative AI creates new content such as text, images, code, audio, or video by learning patterns from training data.

cards.prompt: Define Model in AI and give its practical purpose.
cards.answer: A model is the learned function or system that maps inputs to outputs after training. In AI products, the model is the engine behind predictions or generations.

cards.prompt: Define Algorithm in AI and give its practical purpose.
cards.answer: An algorithm is a precise procedure for solving a problem. In ML it may describe how a model learns, searches, optimizes, or makes predictions.

cards.prompt: Define Dataset in AI and give its practical purpose.
cards.answer: A dataset is a collection of examples used for training, validating, testing, or grounding an AI system.

cards.prompt: Define Feature in AI and give its practical purpose.
cards.answer: A feature is an input signal used by a model, such as a word, pixel value, user action, timestamp, or engineered variable.

cards.prompt: Define Label in AI and give its practical purpose.
cards.answer: A label is the target answer used during supervised learning, such as the correct category, score, translation, or outcome.

cards.prompt: Define Training in AI and give its practical purpose.
cards.answer: Training is the process of adjusting model parameters so outputs become better on examples, usually by minimizing a loss function.

cards.prompt: Define Inference in AI and give its practical purpose.
cards.answer: Inference is using a trained model to produce an output for a new input, such as answering a prompt or classifying an image.

cards.prompt: Define Prompt in AI and give its practical purpose.
cards.answer: A prompt is the instruction and context you give an AI model to shape its response. Good prompts specify goal, context, constraints, examples, and output format.

cards.prompt: Define Completion or response in AI and give its practical purpose.
cards.answer: The completion or response is the model's generated output. It should be judged against the task goal, factuality, usefulness, and constraints.

cards.prompt: Define Token in AI and give its practical purpose.
cards.answer: A token is a unit of text processed by language models. It may be a word, part of a word, punctuation, or whitespace-like unit depending on the tokenizer.

cards.prompt: Define Context window in AI and give its practical purpose.
cards.answer: The context window is the amount of input and prior conversation the model can consider at once. Longer context helps, but irrelevant context can still confuse outputs.

cards.prompt: Define Hallucination in AI and give its practical purpose.
cards.answer: A hallucination is a confident-sounding AI output that is false, unsupported, or fabricated. Reduce it with retrieval, citations, constrained outputs, and verification.

cards.prompt: Define Temperature in AI and give its practical purpose.
cards.answer: Temperature controls randomness in generation. Lower values are more deterministic; higher values increase variety and risk of unexpected outputs.

cards.prompt: Define System prompt in AI and give its practical purpose.
cards.answer: A system prompt gives high-level behavior, role, style, and rules to a model. It should contain durable instructions rather than task-specific one-offs.

cards.prompt: Define User prompt in AI and give its practical purpose.
cards.answer: A user prompt contains the immediate task, question, context, constraints, and desired output for a specific interaction.

cards.prompt: Define Embedding in AI and give its practical purpose.
cards.answer: An embedding is a vector representation of meaning. Similar ideas are placed near each other, enabling semantic search, clustering, and retrieval.

cards.prompt: Define Classification in AI and give its practical purpose.
cards.answer: Classification predicts which category an input belongs to, such as spam versus not spam or beginner versus advanced.

cards.prompt: Define Regression in AI and give its practical purpose.
cards.answer: Regression predicts a continuous number, such as price, demand, probability, time, or score.

cards.prompt: Define Clustering in AI and give its practical purpose.
cards.answer: Clustering groups similar examples without needing labels, often used for exploration, segmentation, or topic discovery.

cards.prompt: Define Supervised learning in AI and give its practical purpose.
cards.answer: Supervised learning trains on examples with known labels so the model can learn to predict labels for new examples.

cards.prompt: Define Unsupervised learning in AI and give its practical purpose.
cards.answer: Unsupervised learning finds patterns in unlabeled data, such as clusters, dimensions, anomalies, or embeddings.

cards.prompt: Define Reinforcement learning in AI and give its practical purpose.
cards.answer: Reinforcement learning trains an agent to choose actions that maximize reward over time in an environment.

cards.prompt: Define Neural network in AI and give its practical purpose.
cards.answer: A neural network is a model made of connected layers of mathematical units that transform inputs into useful outputs.

cards.prompt: Define Loss function in AI and give its practical purpose.
cards.answer: A loss function measures how wrong a model is. Training tries to reduce loss so predictions become better.

cards.prompt: Define Accuracy in AI and give its practical purpose.
cards.answer: Accuracy is the share of predictions that are correct. It can be misleading when classes are imbalanced.

cards.prompt: Define Precision in AI and give its practical purpose.
cards.answer: Precision asks: when the model predicts positive, how often is it right? It matters when false positives are costly.

cards.prompt: Define Recall in AI and give its practical purpose.
cards.answer: Recall asks: of all true positives, how many did the model find? It matters when missing a case is costly.

cards.prompt: Define F1 score in AI and give its practical purpose.
cards.answer: F1 combines precision and recall into one harmonic-mean score, useful when both false positives and false negatives matter.

cards.prompt: Define Overfitting in AI and give its practical purpose.
cards.answer: Overfitting happens when a model memorizes training patterns too narrowly and performs poorly on new data.

cards.prompt: Define Underfitting in AI and give its practical purpose.
cards.answer: Underfitting happens when a model is too simple or poorly trained to capture the real pattern in the data.

cards.prompt: Define Validation set in AI and give its practical purpose.
cards.answer: A validation set is held out during training to tune choices such as parameters, prompts, or model selection without touching the final test set.

cards.prompt: Define Test set in AI and give its practical purpose.
cards.answer: A test set estimates final performance on unseen data after design decisions are made. It should not be used for repeated tuning.

cards.prompt: Define API in AI and give its practical purpose.
cards.answer: An API is an interface that lets software call another service. AI APIs let apps send prompts, files, or data and receive model outputs.

cards.prompt: Define Workflow automation in AI and give its practical purpose.
cards.answer: Workflow automation links tools and steps so repeated work happens reliably, such as collecting input, calling an AI model, checking output, and sending results.

cards.prompt: Define AI assistant in AI and give its practical purpose.
cards.answer: An AI assistant is a system designed to help users complete tasks through conversation, tool use, memory, or workflow execution.

cards.prompt: Define Chatbot in AI and give its practical purpose.
cards.answer: A chatbot is a conversational interface. It may be simple rules-based software or a language-model-powered assistant.

cards.prompt: Explain Transformer and why it matters in applied AI.
cards.answer: A transformer is a neural-network architecture built around attention. It is the foundation for many modern language, vision, and multimodal models.

cards.prompt: Explain Self-attention and why it matters in applied AI.
cards.answer: Self-attention lets each token weigh other tokens in the same context, helping the model decide what information matters for the current representation.

cards.prompt: Explain Multi-head attention and why it matters in applied AI.
cards.answer: Multi-head attention runs attention several ways in parallel so a model can capture different relationships, such as syntax, references, and topic links.

cards.prompt: Explain Positional encoding and why it matters in applied AI.
cards.answer: Positional encoding gives a transformer information about token order, because attention alone does not inherently know sequence position.

cards.prompt: Explain Tokenizer and why it matters in applied AI.
cards.answer: A tokenizer converts text into tokens and token IDs that the model can process. Tokenization affects cost, context length, and exact string handling.

cards.prompt: Explain Vocabulary and why it matters in applied AI.
cards.answer: A model vocabulary is the set of tokens the tokenizer can represent. Unknown words are usually split into smaller known tokens.

cards.prompt: Explain Pretraining and why it matters in applied AI.
cards.answer: Pretraining teaches a model broad patterns from large data before it is adapted for specific tasks or assistant behavior.

cards.prompt: Explain Supervised fine-tuning and why it matters in applied AI.
cards.answer: Supervised fine-tuning trains a pretrained model on curated input-output examples to shape task performance and response style.

cards.prompt: Explain Instruction tuning and why it matters in applied AI.
cards.answer: Instruction tuning adapts a model to follow natural-language instructions across many task types.

cards.prompt: Explain RLHF and why it matters in applied AI.
cards.answer: Reinforcement learning from human feedback uses preference judgments to train or align model behavior toward outputs humans prefer.

cards.prompt: Explain LoRA and why it matters in applied AI.
cards.answer: LoRA is a parameter-efficient fine-tuning method that learns small adapter matrices instead of updating all model weights.

cards.prompt: Explain Quantization and why it matters in applied AI.
cards.answer: Quantization stores or computes model values with lower precision to reduce memory and speed inference, often with some quality tradeoff.

cards.prompt: Explain Batch and why it matters in applied AI.
cards.answer: A batch is a group of examples processed together. Batching can improve hardware efficiency but may affect latency.

cards.prompt: Explain GPU and why it matters in applied AI.
cards.answer: A GPU is a processor optimized for parallel computation, widely used to train and run deep learning models.

cards.prompt: Explain TPU and why it matters in applied AI.
cards.answer: A TPU is a specialized accelerator designed for tensor operations common in machine learning workloads.

cards.prompt: Explain Vector database and why it matters in applied AI.
cards.answer: A vector database stores embeddings and retrieves nearest neighbors, enabling semantic search and RAG systems.

cards.prompt: Explain Cosine similarity and why it matters in applied AI.
cards.answer: Cosine similarity measures the angle between vectors. In embeddings, it is often used to estimate semantic relatedness.

cards.prompt: Explain Semantic search and why it matters in applied AI.
cards.answer: Semantic search retrieves content by meaning instead of exact keywords, usually by comparing embeddings.

cards.prompt: Explain Chunking for RAG and why it matters in applied AI.
cards.answer: Chunking splits documents into smaller units for retrieval. Good chunks preserve meaning, fit context budgets, and avoid splitting important ideas.

cards.prompt: Explain Reranking and why it matters in applied AI.
cards.answer: Reranking reorders retrieved items using a stronger relevance model so the most useful evidence enters the prompt.

cards.prompt: Explain RAG pipeline and why it matters in applied AI.
cards.answer: A RAG pipeline usually ingests documents, chunks them, embeds them, retrieves relevant chunks, builds a grounded prompt, and returns an answer with evidence.

cards.prompt: Explain Grounding and why it matters in applied AI.
cards.answer: Grounding means tying an AI answer to supplied evidence, data, or tools rather than relying only on model memory.

cards.prompt: Explain Citation discipline and why it matters in applied AI.
cards.answer: Citation discipline means every factual claim that needs support is linked to a source or trace, making outputs easier to verify.

cards.prompt: Explain Prompt injection and why it matters in applied AI.
cards.answer: Prompt injection is an attack where untrusted content tries to override instructions or manipulate tool use. Treat external text as data, not commands.

cards.prompt: Explain Jailbreak and why it matters in applied AI.
cards.answer: A jailbreak is an attempt to bypass a model or product's safety and policy constraints through adversarial prompting.

cards.prompt: Explain Guardrails and why it matters in applied AI.
cards.answer: Guardrails are technical and process controls that keep AI outputs within desired rules, such as validation, refusal logic, moderation, and human review.

cards.prompt: Explain Moderation and why it matters in applied AI.
cards.answer: Moderation classifies or filters content to reduce unsafe, abusive, illegal, or policy-violating outputs and inputs.

cards.prompt: Explain Function calling and why it matters in applied AI.
cards.answer: Function calling lets a model request a typed tool or function with structured arguments so software can execute actions reliably.

cards.prompt: Explain JSON schema output and why it matters in applied AI.
cards.answer: JSON schema output constrains model responses to a predictable structure that programs can parse and validate.

cards.prompt: Explain Zero-shot prompting and why it matters in applied AI.
cards.answer: Zero-shot prompting asks a model to perform a task without examples, relying on the instruction alone.

cards.prompt: Explain Few-shot prompting and why it matters in applied AI.
cards.answer: Few-shot prompting includes examples of desired input-output behavior so the model can imitate pattern, style, and structure.

cards.prompt: Explain Chain-of-thought prompting and why it matters in applied AI.
cards.answer: Chain-of-thought prompting asks for step-by-step reasoning, but production systems often prefer concise rationales or hidden reasoning with verifiable final answers.

cards.prompt: Explain Prompt chaining and why it matters in applied AI.
cards.answer: Prompt chaining splits a complex task into smaller model calls, where each step has a clear input, output, and validation rule.

cards.prompt: Explain Evaluation dataset and why it matters in applied AI.
cards.answer: An evaluation dataset is a representative set of tasks and expected outcomes used to measure whether an AI system is improving.

cards.prompt: Explain Benchmark and why it matters in applied AI.
cards.answer: A benchmark is a standardized test for comparing models or systems. It is useful but can miss real workflow performance.

cards.prompt: Explain Confusion matrix and why it matters in applied AI.
cards.answer: A confusion matrix counts true positives, false positives, true negatives, and false negatives for classification analysis.

cards.prompt: Explain ROC AUC and why it matters in applied AI.
cards.answer: ROC AUC summarizes how well a classifier ranks positives above negatives across thresholds.

cards.prompt: Explain Calibration and why it matters in applied AI.
cards.answer: Calibration means predicted probabilities match real-world frequencies. A well-calibrated 80% confidence prediction is right about 80% of the time.

cards.prompt: Explain Data leakage and why it matters in applied AI.
cards.answer: Data leakage occurs when training or evaluation accidentally includes information that would not be available in real use, inflating performance.

cards.prompt: Explain Model drift and why it matters in applied AI.
cards.answer: Model drift happens when real-world data changes so a deployed model becomes less accurate or useful over time.

cards.prompt: Explain Latency and why it matters in applied AI.
cards.answer: Latency is the time from request to response. AI latency is affected by model size, context length, tools, network, and output length.

cards.prompt: Explain Throughput and why it matters in applied AI.
cards.answer: Throughput is how many requests or tokens a system can process per unit of time.

cards.prompt: Explain Cost per token and why it matters in applied AI.
cards.answer: Cost per token estimates model usage cost based on input and output token counts, often critical for production economics.

cards.prompt: Explain Prompt caching and why it matters in applied AI.
cards.answer: Prompt caching reuses repeated prompt prefixes or context to reduce latency and cost when supported by the provider.

cards.prompt: Explain Fine-tuning vs RAG and why it matters in applied AI.
cards.answer: RAG adds external knowledge at inference time; fine-tuning changes model behavior or specialized patterns. Use RAG for fresh facts, fine-tuning for consistent behavior.

cards.prompt: Explain API key and why it matters in applied AI.
cards.answer: An API key authenticates software to a service. Treat it like a secret: never commit it to GitHub or expose it in client-side code.

cards.prompt: What is Agent, and what failure does it help prevent or solve?
cards.answer: An agent is an AI system that can plan, call tools, maintain state, and take multiple steps toward a goal under constraints.

cards.prompt: What is Planner-executor architecture, and what failure does it help prevent or solve?
cards.answer: A planner-executor pattern separates strategy from action: one component decomposes the task, another executes steps and reports results.

cards.prompt: What is Tool router, and what failure does it help prevent or solve?
cards.answer: A tool router decides which tool or API should handle a subtask, based on intent, permissions, input type, and reliability.

cards.prompt: What is State machine, and what failure does it help prevent or solve?
cards.answer: A state machine models allowed states and transitions so an AI workflow behaves predictably instead of drifting through vague conversation.

cards.prompt: What is Semantic memory, and what failure does it help prevent or solve?
cards.answer: Semantic memory stores durable facts, concepts, and preferences that can be retrieved later for context.

cards.prompt: What is Episodic memory, and what failure does it help prevent or solve?
cards.answer: Episodic memory stores event-like records of what happened in past interactions, including decisions, outcomes, and timestamps.

cards.prompt: What is Procedural memory, and what failure does it help prevent or solve?
cards.answer: Procedural memory stores reusable methods, playbooks, skills, and workflows rather than facts.

cards.prompt: What is Scratchpad, and what failure does it help prevent or solve?
cards.answer: A scratchpad is temporary working memory for intermediate notes, calculations, or step results. It should not replace verified final outputs.

cards.prompt: What is Observability, and what failure does it help prevent or solve?
cards.answer: Observability means logging prompts, tool calls, traces, latency, costs, errors, and outcomes so AI systems can be debugged and improved.

cards.prompt: What is Tracing, and what failure does it help prevent or solve?
cards.answer: Tracing records the steps in an AI workflow, including model calls, tool calls, inputs, outputs, and timing.

cards.prompt: What is Evaluation harness, and what failure does it help prevent or solve?
cards.answer: An evaluation harness automatically runs test cases against prompts, models, and workflows to detect regressions and compare variants.

cards.prompt: What is Golden dataset, and what failure does it help prevent or solve?
cards.answer: A golden dataset is a carefully reviewed set of examples and expected outputs used as a stable quality reference.

cards.prompt: What is Prompt unit test, and what failure does it help prevent or solve?
cards.answer: A prompt unit test checks whether a prompt still produces acceptable behavior on representative cases after changes.

cards.prompt: What is Red-teaming, and what failure does it help prevent or solve?
cards.answer: Red-teaming deliberately probes a system for failures, misuse, bias, leakage, or unsafe behavior before deployment.

cards.prompt: What is Human-in-the-loop, and what failure does it help prevent or solve?
cards.answer: Human-in-the-loop design sends uncertain, high-risk, or high-value decisions to people for review, approval, or correction.

cards.prompt: What is Confidence threshold, and what failure does it help prevent or solve?
cards.answer: A confidence threshold triggers different actions depending on estimated reliability, such as answer, ask, retrieve more, or escalate.

cards.prompt: What is Fallback model, and what failure does it help prevent or solve?
cards.answer: A fallback model or path handles failures, overload, cost spikes, safety issues, or low confidence from the primary route.

cards.prompt: What is Retrieval precision, and what failure does it help prevent or solve?
cards.answer: Retrieval precision is the share of retrieved chunks that are actually relevant. Low precision pollutes the model context.

cards.prompt: What is Retrieval recall, and what failure does it help prevent or solve?
cards.answer: Retrieval recall is the share of needed evidence that the retriever found. Low recall means the answer may miss crucial facts.

cards.prompt: What is Chunk overlap, and what failure does it help prevent or solve?
cards.answer: Chunk overlap repeats some text between adjacent chunks to avoid cutting important meaning at boundaries.

cards.prompt: What is Hybrid search, and what failure does it help prevent or solve?
cards.answer: Hybrid search combines keyword retrieval such as BM25 with vector retrieval so exact terms and semantic meaning both matter.

cards.prompt: What is BM25, and what failure does it help prevent or solve?
cards.answer: BM25 is a classic keyword ranking method that scores documents by term frequency, inverse document frequency, and length normalization.

cards.prompt: What is Knowledge graph, and what failure does it help prevent or solve?
cards.answer: A knowledge graph represents entities and relationships explicitly, helping systems reason about connected facts and dependencies.

cards.prompt: What is MCP, and what failure does it help prevent or solve?
cards.answer: Model Context Protocol is a standard pattern for connecting AI applications to external tools, data sources, and actions through servers.

cards.prompt: What is Webhook, and what failure does it help prevent or solve?
cards.answer: A webhook sends data from one system to another when an event happens, enabling event-driven AI automations.

cards.prompt: What is OAuth scopes, and what failure does it help prevent or solve?
cards.answer: OAuth scopes limit what an integration can access or do. Least-privilege scopes reduce damage from mistakes or compromise.

cards.prompt: What is Secrets management, and what failure does it help prevent or solve?
cards.answer: Secrets management protects API keys, tokens, and credentials using environment variables or secret stores rather than source code.

cards.prompt: What is Rate limiting, and what failure does it help prevent or solve?
cards.answer: Rate limiting controls how many requests can be made in a time window, protecting services from overload and abuse.

cards.prompt: What is Retry with backoff, and what failure does it help prevent or solve?
cards.answer: Retry with backoff waits longer between retries after failures, reducing overload and improving reliability.

cards.prompt: What is Queue, and what failure does it help prevent or solve?
cards.answer: A queue stores jobs so work can be processed asynchronously, smoothed over spikes, and retried safely.

cards.prompt: What is CI/CD, and what failure does it help prevent or solve?
cards.answer: CI/CD automates testing, building, and deployment so code and AI workflow changes can ship with fewer regressions.

cards.prompt: What is Docker, and what failure does it help prevent or solve?
cards.answer: Docker packages software with its runtime environment so it runs more consistently across machines and servers.

cards.prompt: What is Git, and what failure does it help prevent or solve?
cards.answer: Git tracks code changes over time, enabling branching, review, rollback, and collaboration.

cards.prompt: What is GitHub, and what failure does it help prevent or solve?
cards.answer: GitHub hosts Git repositories and supports pull requests, issues, actions, and collaboration around code.

cards.prompt: What is GitHub Actions, and what failure does it help prevent or solve?
cards.answer: GitHub Actions runs automated workflows such as tests, deployments, linting, and scheduled jobs inside GitHub.

cards.prompt: What is OpenAI Codex, and what failure does it help prevent or solve?
cards.answer: Codex is OpenAI's coding agent family for editing, explaining, running, and reviewing code in local, cloud, IDE, CLI, and GitHub workflows.

cards.prompt: What is GitHub Copilot, and what failure does it help prevent or solve?
cards.answer: GitHub Copilot is an AI coding assistant integrated into developer workflows for code suggestions, chat, and repository-aware help.

cards.prompt: What is Cursor, and what failure does it help prevent or solve?
cards.answer: Cursor is an AI-focused code editor that lets developers work with codebases through chat, edits, and agentic workflows.

cards.prompt: What is VS Code, and what failure does it help prevent or solve?
cards.answer: VS Code is a widely used code editor with extensions, terminal integration, debugging, and AI coding assistant support.

cards.prompt: What is Hugging Face, and what failure does it help prevent or solve?
cards.answer: Hugging Face is an ecosystem for models, datasets, Spaces apps, evaluation tools, and open-source ML collaboration.

cards.prompt: What is LangChain, and what failure does it help prevent or solve?
cards.answer: LangChain is a framework for building LLM applications with chains, agents, tools, retrievers, and integrations.

cards.prompt: What is LlamaIndex, and what failure does it help prevent or solve?
cards.answer: LlamaIndex focuses on connecting private or domain data to LLM applications, especially retrieval and data indexing workflows.

cards.prompt: What is FAISS, and what failure does it help prevent or solve?
cards.answer: FAISS is a library for efficient similarity search over vectors, commonly used for embedding retrieval.

cards.prompt: What is Chroma, and what failure does it help prevent or solve?
cards.answer: Chroma is a developer-friendly vector database often used for local RAG prototypes and embedding search.

cards.prompt: What is Pinecone, and what failure does it help prevent or solve?
cards.answer: Pinecone is a managed vector database for production semantic search and RAG systems.

cards.prompt: What is MLflow, and what failure does it help prevent or solve?
cards.answer: MLflow tracks experiments, models, parameters, metrics, and deployment artifacts across the ML lifecycle.

cards.prompt: What is Weights and Biases, and what failure does it help prevent or solve?
cards.answer: Weights and Biases helps track experiments, datasets, prompts, traces, and model evaluation metrics.

cards.prompt: Give a precise explanation of Scaling laws and one implication for building AI systems.
cards.answer: Scaling laws describe predictable relationships between model performance and factors such as compute, data, and parameter count.

cards.prompt: Give a precise explanation of Compute optimal training and one implication for building AI systems.
cards.answer: Compute optimal training balances model size, data size, and compute budget so resources improve capability efficiently.

cards.prompt: Give a precise explanation of Distillation and one implication for building AI systems.
cards.answer: Distillation trains a smaller or cheaper model to imitate a larger model, often improving latency and cost while retaining useful behavior.

cards.prompt: Give a precise explanation of Mixture of experts and one implication for building AI systems.
cards.answer: A mixture-of-experts model routes inputs through selected expert subnetworks, increasing capacity while activating only part of the model per token.

cards.prompt: Give a precise explanation of Sparse activation and one implication for building AI systems.
cards.answer: Sparse activation means only some parameters or experts are used for a given input, saving compute compared with dense activation.

cards.prompt: Give a precise explanation of KV cache and one implication for building AI systems.
cards.answer: A KV cache stores key and value tensors from previous tokens so autoregressive inference can avoid recomputing the entire context.

cards.prompt: Give a precise explanation of Speculative decoding and one implication for building AI systems.
cards.answer: Speculative decoding uses a smaller draft model to propose tokens that a larger model verifies, often speeding generation.

cards.prompt: Give a precise explanation of Long-context modeling and one implication for building AI systems.
cards.answer: Long-context modeling lets systems use more documents or conversation history, but quality still depends on retrieval, attention, and relevance management.

cards.prompt: Give a precise explanation of Attention optimization and one implication for building AI systems.
cards.answer: Attention optimization reduces the compute or memory cost of attention, helping long-context and high-throughput inference.

cards.prompt: Give a precise explanation of Multimodal model and one implication for building AI systems.
cards.answer: A multimodal model can process or generate across modalities such as text, images, audio, video, and structured data.

cards.prompt: Give a precise explanation of Vision-language model and one implication for building AI systems.
cards.answer: A vision-language model connects images and text, enabling tasks such as image description, visual question answering, and document understanding.

cards.prompt: Give a precise explanation of Diffusion model and one implication for building AI systems.
cards.answer: A diffusion model generates data by learning to reverse a gradual noising process, widely used in image, audio, and video generation.

cards.prompt: Give a precise explanation of Speech-to-text and one implication for building AI systems.
cards.answer: Speech-to-text converts audio speech into written text, often used for transcription, voice assistants, and searchable meeting notes.

cards.prompt: Give a precise explanation of Text-to-speech and one implication for building AI systems.
cards.answer: Text-to-speech converts written text into spoken audio, enabling voice agents, accessibility, and content production.

cards.prompt: Give a precise explanation of Synthetic data and one implication for building AI systems.
cards.answer: Synthetic data is generated rather than directly collected. It can augment training or testing but must be checked for bias, leakage, and realism.

cards.prompt: Give a precise explanation of Evaluation contamination and one implication for building AI systems.
cards.answer: Evaluation contamination occurs when benchmark answers or near-duplicates appear in training data, making results look better than real generalization.

cards.prompt: Give a precise explanation of Adversarial example and one implication for building AI systems.
cards.answer: An adversarial example is an input crafted to make a model fail, often revealing brittleness or unsafe behavior.

cards.prompt: Give a precise explanation of Interpretability and one implication for building AI systems.
cards.answer: Interpretability studies how to understand why a model produced an output, from feature importance to mechanistic circuit analysis.

cards.prompt: Give a precise explanation of Mechanistic interpretability and one implication for building AI systems.
cards.answer: Mechanistic interpretability tries to reverse-engineer internal model circuits and representations to understand behavior at a detailed level.

cards.prompt: Give a precise explanation of Activation and one implication for building AI systems.
cards.answer: An activation is the numeric output of a model unit or layer for a given input. Activations are internal signals used to compute predictions.

cards.prompt: Give a precise explanation of Logits and one implication for building AI systems.
cards.answer: Logits are raw model scores before probability conversion. In language models, logits are converted into next-token probabilities.

cards.prompt: Give a precise explanation of Softmax and one implication for building AI systems.
cards.answer: Softmax converts logits into probabilities that sum to one, often used for classification or next-token prediction.

cards.prompt: Give a precise explanation of Top-k sampling and one implication for building AI systems.
cards.answer: Top-k sampling limits generation to the k most likely tokens before sampling, reducing very unlikely continuations.

cards.prompt: Give a precise explanation of Top-p sampling and one implication for building AI systems.
cards.answer: Top-p sampling, also called nucleus sampling, chooses from the smallest token set whose cumulative probability exceeds p.

cards.prompt: Give a precise explanation of Beam search and one implication for building AI systems.
cards.answer: Beam search keeps several high-scoring candidate sequences during decoding, often useful in structured generation but less natural for open chat.

cards.prompt: Give a precise explanation of Reward model and one implication for building AI systems.
cards.answer: A reward model predicts which outputs are preferable, enabling reinforcement-learning style optimization from human or AI feedback.

cards.prompt: Give a precise explanation of RLAIF and one implication for building AI systems.
cards.answer: Reinforcement learning from AI feedback uses AI-generated preference or critique signals instead of, or alongside, human feedback.

cards.prompt: Give a precise explanation of Constitutional AI and one implication for building AI systems.
cards.answer: Constitutional AI uses a written set of principles to guide critique and revision of model outputs during training or alignment.

cards.prompt: Give a precise explanation of Alignment and one implication for building AI systems.
cards.answer: Alignment is the effort to make AI systems act according to intended goals, human values, safety constraints, and truthful behavior.

cards.prompt: Give a precise explanation of Differential privacy and one implication for building AI systems.
cards.answer: Differential privacy adds mathematical noise or constraints so aggregate learning reveals less about any single person's data.

cards.prompt: Give a precise explanation of Federated learning and one implication for building AI systems.
cards.answer: Federated learning trains across devices or organizations while keeping raw data local, sharing updates instead of centralizing data.

cards.prompt: Give a precise explanation of Model card and one implication for building AI systems.
cards.answer: A model card documents a model's intended uses, limitations, training data notes, evaluation results, and risks.

cards.prompt: Give a precise explanation of System card and one implication for building AI systems.
cards.answer: A system card documents a deployed AI system's capabilities, limitations, safety mitigations, evaluations, and release considerations.

cards.prompt: Give a precise explanation of Data governance and one implication for building AI systems.
cards.answer: Data governance defines who can access data, how it is classified, retained, audited, and used safely in AI systems.

cards.prompt: Give a precise explanation of Inference serving and one implication for building AI systems.
cards.answer: Inference serving is the production infrastructure for receiving requests, running models, applying tools or guardrails, and returning outputs reliably.

cards.prompt: Give a precise explanation of Agentic evaluation and one implication for building AI systems.
cards.answer: Agentic evaluation tests multi-step AI systems on goals, tool use, recovery from errors, side effects, and final outcomes.

cards.prompt: What are the four parts of an RTCF-style prompt?
cards.answer: Role, task, context, and format. A strong prompt says who the model should act as, what it must do, what information it should use, and exactly how the answer should be shaped.

cards.prompt: How do you make an AI answer easier to reuse in another tool?
cards.answer: Specify the exact structure: bullets, table, JSON, checklist, title length, fields, or examples. Add constraints such as 'return only valid JSON' when software will parse the answer.

cards.prompt: Write the pattern for using AI as an active-recall tutor.
cards.answer: Ask the AI to quiz you one question at a time, wait for your answer, grade it against a rubric, correct gaps, and schedule the next review.

cards.prompt: When should you use examples in a prompt?
cards.answer: Use examples when output style, classification boundaries, or edge cases are hard to describe in rules alone. Keep examples concise and representative.

cards.prompt: How do you prompt a model for a complex task without overloading it?
cards.answer: Break the work into smaller steps: define goal, gather facts, create outline, draft, critique, revise, and validate. Each step should have a clear output.

cards.prompt: What should a verification prompt ask the AI to do?
cards.answer: Ask it to list claims, identify unsupported claims, check calculations, cite evidence, flag uncertainty, and separate facts from assumptions.

cards.prompt: What must a tool-use prompt specify to reduce bad actions?
cards.answer: It should specify allowed tools, when to use each tool, required arguments, permission boundaries, validation checks, and when to ask for human approval.

cards.prompt: Why is a rubric useful for AI outputs?
cards.answer: A rubric turns subjective quality into explicit criteria, enabling consistent grading, self-review, evals, and improvement loops.

cards.prompt: What is context engineering?
cards.answer: Context engineering is designing what information, tools, memories, policies, examples, and state enter the model context so the system behaves reliably.

cards.prompt: What is eval-first prompting?
cards.answer: Eval-first prompting means building representative tests before optimizing prompts, so changes are measured against real use cases instead of vibes.

cards.prompt: What is ChatGPT used for in an AI learning or build workflow?
cards.answer: AI assistant for conversation, writing, analysis, coding help, multimodal tasks, and workflow support.

cards.prompt: What is OpenAI API used for in an AI learning or build workflow?
cards.answer: Developer platform for adding model calls, structured outputs, tools, audio, vision, and agents to applications.

cards.prompt: What is Claude used for in an AI learning or build workflow?
cards.answer: Anthropic's assistant family used for writing, coding, reasoning, document work, and tool-using workflows.

cards.prompt: What is Claude Code used for in an AI learning or build workflow?
cards.answer: Agentic coding tool from Anthropic for working with codebases through terminal or editor workflows.

cards.prompt: What is Gemini used for in an AI learning or build workflow?
cards.answer: Google's AI model and assistant ecosystem for multimodal reasoning, coding, search-connected tasks, and Workspace integrations.

cards.prompt: What is NotebookLM used for in an AI learning or build workflow?
cards.answer: Google tool for working with sources, summaries, study guides, and grounded notes from uploaded materials.

cards.prompt: What is Perplexity used for in an AI learning or build workflow?
cards.answer: AI answer engine focused on web-connected research with citations and source discovery.

cards.prompt: What is GitHub Copilot used for in an AI learning or build workflow?
cards.answer: AI pair programmer integrated into GitHub and editors for suggestions, chat, and coding help.

cards.prompt: What is OpenAI Codex CLI used for in an AI learning or build workflow?
cards.answer: Local terminal coding agent that can inspect repositories, edit files, and run commands.

cards.prompt: What is Hugging Face Hub used for in an AI learning or build workflow?
cards.answer: Repository ecosystem for models, datasets, demos, and ML collaboration.

cards.prompt: What is Google Colab used for in an AI learning or build workflow?
cards.answer: Hosted notebook environment for Python, data science, and GPU-backed ML experiments.

cards.prompt: What is Kaggle used for in an AI learning or build workflow?
cards.answer: Platform for datasets, notebooks, ML competitions, and example projects.

cards.prompt: What is Jupyter used for in an AI learning or build workflow?
cards.answer: Interactive notebook environment for code, charts, data exploration, and reproducible analysis.

cards.prompt: What is Ollama used for in an AI learning or build workflow?
cards.answer: Local model runner for using open-weight language models on your own machine.

cards.prompt: What is LM Studio used for in an AI learning or build workflow?
cards.answer: Desktop app for discovering, downloading, and running local language models.

cards.prompt: What is vLLM used for in an AI learning or build workflow?
cards.answer: High-throughput inference server/library for serving language models efficiently.

cards.prompt: What is LangSmith used for in an AI learning or build workflow?
cards.answer: Observability and evaluation platform for LLM applications, especially LangChain workflows.

cards.prompt: What is Pinecone used for in an AI learning or build workflow?
cards.answer: Managed vector database for production semantic search and RAG.

cards.prompt: What is Weaviate used for in an AI learning or build workflow?
cards.answer: Vector database with hybrid search and retrieval features for AI applications.

cards.prompt: What is Zapier used for in an AI learning or build workflow?
cards.answer: No-code automation platform connecting apps and AI steps.

cards.prompt: What is Make used for in an AI learning or build workflow?
cards.answer: Visual automation platform for building workflows across apps and APIs.

cards.prompt: What is n8n used for in an AI learning or build workflow?
cards.answer: Source-available workflow automation tool popular for self-hosted AI automations.

cards.prompt: What is Postman used for in an AI learning or build workflow?
cards.answer: API development and testing tool for sending requests, documenting endpoints, and debugging integrations.

cards.prompt: What is Docker used for in an AI learning or build workflow?
cards.answer: Container platform that makes projects easier to run consistently across machines.

chunks.title: AI vocabulary spine
chunks.goal: Build instant recall for core terms: AI, ML, model, data, prompt, tokens, context, hallucination.

chunks.title: ML task types
chunks.goal: Separate classification, regression, clustering, supervised, unsupervised, and reinforcement learning.

chunks.title: Evaluation basics
chunks.goal: Know accuracy, precision, recall, F1, overfitting, underfitting, validation, and test sets.

chunks.title: LLM architecture
chunks.goal: Understand tokens, transformers, attention, positional encoding, pretraining, and tuning.

chunks.title: RAG and retrieval
chunks.goal: Connect embeddings, vector databases, semantic search, chunking, reranking, grounding, and citations.

chunks.title: Prompting operator basics
chunks.goal: Use RTCF, examples, decomposition, verification, and output schemas.

chunks.title: Agent systems
chunks.goal: Map agents, planners, routers, state machines, memory, scratchpads, and tool prompts.

chunks.title: Production reliability
chunks.goal: Build around evals, observability, traces, fallbacks, rate limits, retries, queues, CI/CD.

chunks.title: Security and safety
chunks.goal: Recognize prompt injection, jailbreaks, secrets, OAuth scopes, red-teaming, human review, moderation.

chunks.title: Frontier internals
chunks.goal: Understand logits, softmax, sampling, KV cache, speculative decoding, MoE, scaling laws.

chunks.title: Governance and alignment
chunks.goal: Connect model cards, system cards, data governance, privacy, alignment, constitutional AI, and evaluations.

promptLabs.title: Turn a vague prompt into a precise task
promptLabs.mission: Rewrite it so the model teaches AI foundations to a busy beginner in 15 minutes.
promptLabs.model: Act as an AI tutor. Teach a busy beginner the 12 most important AI foundations in 15 minutes. Use a table with term, plain-English meaning, example, and one active-recall question. End with a 5-question quiz and answer key.

promptLabs.title: Make a RAG answer verifiable
promptLabs.mission: Rewrite it so the answer is grounded and cites evidence.
promptLabs.model: Use only the provided documents. For each factual claim, cite the document title and section. If evidence is missing, say 'not found in sources'. Finish with assumptions, open questions, and a confidence level.

promptLabs.title: Structure output for automation
promptLabs.bad: Summarize these leads and tell me who to contact.
promptLabs.mission: Rewrite it so another tool can parse the output.
promptLabs.model: Return only valid JSON with an array named leads. Each lead must include name, company, fit_score_0_to_100, reason, missing_info, next_action, and draft_email. Rank by fit_score descending.

promptLabs.title: Safe tool-use prompt
promptLabs.bad: Use my tools to fix this customer issue.
promptLabs.mission: Rewrite it to include permission boundaries and escalation rules.
promptLabs.model: You may inspect CRM notes, draft replies, and create a proposed refund note. Do not send messages, issue refunds, delete data, or change account status without explicit human approval. Log each tool call, validate customer ID before action, and escalate if confidence is below 0.8.

promptLabs.title: Eval-first optimization
promptLabs.bad: Improve my prompt until it feels good.
promptLabs.mission: Rewrite it so improvements are measured against a test set.
promptLabs.model: Create 20 representative test cases first. Define pass/fail criteria for factuality, instruction following, format validity, and safety. Then propose three prompt variants, run them against the tests, summarize failures, and recommend the smallest high-confidence change.

sessions: Install/run app locally or via GitHub Pages

studyPlan.focus: ML task types and evaluation basics
sessions: Write your own examples for classification/regression/clustering
Create 5 prompts for your own workflow
Map when to use each tool
Design an AI learning assistant architecture
